skill.name: functional-patterns
skill.description: Functional programming patterns that promote testability, composability, and maintainability.
skill.summary: Functional programming patterns that promote testability, composability, and maintainability.
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported markdown backticks as Ruby shell execution, but the locations are TypeScript documentation code fences, not executable skill logic. Weak crypto and reconnaissance alerts were also false positives from ordinary prose, test syntax, console logging, in-memory lookup, and logger examples; no prompt injection, exfiltration, network call, or runnable script was found.

low_findings.title: Static external command detections are markdown code fences
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged TypeScript fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The cited lines are documentation delimiters and examples, not executable commands run by the skill.
low_findings.recommendation: No security change is required. Keep examples as documentation and do not add executable scripts without a separate review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a markdown TypeScript fence or example line in SKILL.md. No runnable shell command, script file, or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are substring false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not identify hashing or encryption code. The cited content is the skill description and a Jest describe block.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required unless future revisions add real cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain metadata description text and test syntax, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar algorithms. There is no cryptographic implementation in the skill.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance detections are ordinary TypeScript examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts map to console logging, logger usage, and in-memory user lookup examples. They do not inspect the host system or enumerate files, processes, users, or environment data.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is required. Review future changes if examples start reading host system state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are instructional examples for logging and data lookup. I found no system commands, filesystem enumeration, environment access, or network discovery behavior.

content.value_statement: Node.js and TypeScript services can become hard to test when logic and side effects are mixed. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward pure functions, immutable updates, composition, Result types, Option types, and function-based dependency injection.
actual_capabilities: Explains pure functions and separates pure business logic from impure I/O shells.
Shows immutable object and array update patterns for TypeScript code.
Demonstrates pipe, compose, higher-order functions, currying, and partial application.
Provides Result and Option patterns for explicit error and absence handling.
Shows function-based dependency injection for testable Node.js services.
limitations: Does not include executable scripts, generators, or automated refactoring tools.
Does not cover functional libraries such as fp-ts in depth.
Examples are illustrative and may need project-specific types before use.
Does not replace security or performance review for production code.

use_cases.title: Refactor Service Logic
use_cases.description: Separate validation, calculation, and persistence so service behavior becomes easier to test.

use_cases.title: Teach Functional Patterns
use_cases.description: Use concise TypeScript examples to explain pure functions, immutability, composition, Result, and Option patterns.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Identify mutable state, hidden side effects, and exception-heavy flows that can be expressed more clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the pure core and impure shell pattern for this TypeScript service. Keep the explanation practical and include one small before-and-after example.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Mutable Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript function and refactor it to avoid mutation. Preserve behavior, improve type clarity, and explain the key changes.

prompt_templates.title: Add Result Handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Replace thrown errors with explicit results
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this validation and processing flow to use a Result type. Show how callers should handle both success and failure paths.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Testable Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Node.js TypeScript service using function-based dependency injection, a pure business core, Result errors, and isolated I/O boundaries.

output_examples.input: A checkout handler mixes validation, discount calculation, database writes, and notification sending.
output_examples.output: The response separates validation and discount calculation into pure functions. It keeps database and notification calls in a small handler layer.

output_examples.input: A user lookup returns null and callers repeatedly check for missing values.
output_examples.output: The response models absence with an Option value and centralizes fallback behavior with a clear default path.

output_examples.input: A service directly imports repositories, a logger, and the system clock.
output_examples.output: The response introduces a dependencies object so tests can pass fake repositories, a fake logger, and a fixed clock.

best_practices: Keep business rules pure and pass side-effecting dependencies at the boundary.
Prefer immutable updates when returning changed objects or arrays.
Use Result or Option types when failure or absence is expected.
anti_patterns: Do not hide I/O inside functions that should be deterministic.
Do not mutate input objects when callers expect reusable values.
Do not add complex abstractions when simple typed functions are enough.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with TypeScript examples and guidance.

faq.question: Which languages does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Node.js and TypeScript.

faq.question: Can it help with testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It promotes pure functions and dependency injection patterns that make unit tests simpler.

faq.question: Does it require a functional programming library?
faq.answer: No. The examples use plain TypeScript types and functions.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production code?
faq.answer: Yes, but examples should be adapted to project conventions and reviewed like any production change.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it for refactoring?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompts and examples guide agents toward small, testable functional refactors.
